flashcards.front: What is a bus in a computer system?
flashcards.back: A bus is a group of parallel wires (lines) that serves as a shared communication path connecting the CPU, memory, and I/O devices for transferring data, addresses, and control signals.

flashcards.front: Name the three types of buses in a computer system.
flashcards.back:
1. Data Bus (bidirectional, carries data)
2. Address Bus (unidirectional, carries memory addresses from CPU)
3. Control Bus (carries timing and control signals like Read/Write, clock, interrupts)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a synchronous and an asynchronous bus?
flashcards.back:
Synchronous bus: Uses a common clock signal; transfers take a fixed number of clock cycles. Simple but limited by slowest device.
Asynchronous bus: Uses handshaking protocol (request/acknowledge); can accommodate devices of different speeds. More complex but more flexible.

flashcards.front: What is bus arbitration and why is it needed?
flashcards.back: Bus arbitration is the process of deciding which device gets access to the bus when multiple devices request it simultaneously. It is needed because only one device can use the shared bus at a time. Methods include centralized (single arbiter) and distributed (each device has its own arbitration logic).
Bus Bandwidth = Bus Width (bits) x Bus Clock Frequency (Hz). Example: A 64-bit bus at 800 MHz has bandwidth = 64 x 800M = 51,200 Mbit/s = 6,400 MB/s.
